sentences.text: Yes.
[SIGHS] I had so much fear in me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I tried to be quiet.
But as a child, you don't always do what you're supposed to do.
But I also remember hearing stories of a lot of mothers who dropped their children off at a lot of the farm places, hoping that if they don't survive, maybe the farm lady was a good person.
And she will take this child.
And, and hopefully, maybe this child will live.
I heard those stories.
And I was so afraid that my mother would do this to me.
So when I held her hand, my mother said that my nails dug into her because I was so afraid she would let go.
And I hung on to it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My mother would never let go, never.
She said I was her reason for living.
And she had to fight on.
She had to have something left from her life.
Whatever my mother could scrounge from the animals that they left.
And, like I said, whatever-- if it rained, we were lucky.
We had water to drink from.
We had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not much food, very little food.
Very hungry.
The hunger is like a knot in your stomach that's craving for something.
I remember walking in the night and seeing a, a farmhouse.
And I looked in the window.
And I saw a family sitting at a table.
And I said, [SOBBING] Mama, someday, can I be doing this?
Will I sit at a table again, like we used to, like those people, instead of all this running all the time-- and hiding and running?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And she says, yes, yes, my Kind, yes, my child.
You'll see.
We're going to make it-- positive, a very courageous, courageous woman.
We were in the woods.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And a lot of people were there, hiding like us.
But a lot of them, what they did is they dug holes in the woods, you know, an area for them to sit in.
Their hands, whatever they had-- pieces of wood, whatever they could.
And they kept digging and digging.
At night.
And then they would-- I remember a part of the grass would be put over the top so that it wouldn't be noticeable.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Mother couldn't dig the hole for us to hide in.
And nobody would take us in because of me.
Nobody wanted a child to be there.
They were afraid I would cry or something and give them away when the Germans came searching through the woods.
So my mother was sitting by a tree.
And she said, OK, don't take me in.
But, she says, the first German that comes to me, I'm going to point my finger where the rest of you are.
And I'm not going to be taken away alone.
So one father and his daughter decided he's not taking a chance on my mother.
She only said that.
She would never do that.
She wasn't that type of a person.
But she didn't know what to do.
So he said, OK, you come into the hole that we're in.
And it was that hole that was, like, our home for, I don't know, so many months, whatever it is.
And it was just a dark [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] hole in the ground with worms and things crawling in there.
And they were-- took some-- got into my skin.
And I remember the lice that I had in my hair that was constantly crawling.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I, I didn't know.
I says, Mama.
And she would just do the best she could.
And we kept sitting in there.
And at night, my mother would crawl out and try and get a little bit of food.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Four of us.
It was this-- oh, while we're talking about this, this father and daughter that were in this hole that took us in, I must just tell you-- I had-- I was watching a program on television.
And it was with Barbara Walters.
And it was Spielberg's-- who taped a lot of these documentaries that you people are doing right now.
And as I'm watching it, they're interviewing this older person.
And it doesn't ring any bells to me at all.
But then they showed a passport of this woman.
And she was younger.
And all of a sudden, like, a light goes on in your head.
I screamed out to my husband, that's [?
Chaja! ?] This is the woman and her father that dug that hole that took us in.
And it was, like, I couldn't believe I was really seeing it.
But this is the wonderful work that Spielberg foundation is doing.
It's-- I just think it's wonderful.
Anyways, to go on from then on, it was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] tall fields of corn-- grows very tall.
And that was another hiding place.
And we were there.
And I remember there was another woman and her child sitting there.
And my mother says, Tama, [NON-ENGLISH], come here.
And as I did, a bullet went through her shoulder.
It was another time that I was spared.
And then a lot of bullets were-- like machine guns going off.
And one of them grazed my left thigh at that time.
And blood was coming out all over the place.
And my mother, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she did the best she could.
She tore off a piece of her dress and tried wrapping it up.
But it kept coming through.
And she tried healing it.
She got whatever she could to put on it to heal it.
And that was our-- another close encounter.
And then we were running again.
That's all I remember in my life as a youngster.
It's just the dark and the fear and the running-- and just running and running.
And we ran.
And all of a sudden, this German fellow with a gun-- halt!
[POINTS] That's all.
You know, with a gun like this at us.
I don't know where he came from.
But there he was.
And my mother looked at him.
And he-- and she pleaded for her life and my life.
And she, in Jewish, said to me, cry.
See I-- well, being a child, I didn't want to cry at that particular moment, not realizing the terrible situation we were in.
So I remember my mother pinching me so hard.
Well, I started to cry.
And I cried.
And she said, please, you must have a family.
Look at this child.
She is crying.
How can you possibly kill this child and me?
I did nothing to you.
What have you got to gain by killing us?
And I cried and cried.
And he looked at me.
And she says, surely, you must have children.
And he says, I do.
Take your child and run.
And we ran again.
We just ran and ran.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS]
Two years.
The seasons changed.
My mother's feet were starting to freeze and mine.
And she took as much clothing off of her as possible and wrapped my feet.
And then some kind-hearted farm ladies gave her some rags and stuff.
So she covered up my hands and feet and some of hers.
And we just kept running.
Constantly.
Constantly cold because my teeth were always, you know--
--chattering.
Cold and dirty and hungry.
Sometimes.
They had signs up on their houses that said, if you harbor a Jew, not only are we going to kill the Jew, but you as well.
So they were petrified.
Some of them--
Oh, they were written right on the houses.
And they posted them everywhere.
And they were afraid.
And some of them wanted to help.
But they were afraid.
And this is how we tried to survive.
This is how we carried on.
I think so.
Must have.
I mean, they kept seeing a lot of it disappear, all the, the slop and the stuff that they throw out to the pigs and the animals.
And a lot of it was taken.
They used to throw out a lot of boiled potatoes, I remember.
I ate a lot of potatoes, you know, because my mother would gather up whatever she could in her clothing and come running, like a bird runs to her a little child with some food.
Whatever she could find.
She'd go into the garden.
She'd pull up some radishes or, or a carrot or whatever she could find, whatever she could do.
Only at night.
You were hungry, very hungry.
Yes, no, I-- she would always keep me with her except at night, when she would go looking for food.
She would leave me in that hole.
And I would cry.
I was so afraid she wouldn't come back.
But she says, I'm going to come back.
And I had to stay there.
While we were in the woods, I was with that other person I mentioned to you and her father.
We were there.
And-- or else he would go, and she would be there.
Somebody stayed there.
That's all we ever got was-- the partisan people, I remember them talking that things are, are getting a little better.
They're, they're-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they used to-- they, too, were fighting and, and hiding.
But they-- I only-- this is only something that I would hear occasionally.
And they said the partisan was coming and not to be afraid of the partisans because they're not going to hurt us.
Yes.
I remember seeing them dressed in very drab, green clothing, like.
And they would carry-- some would have rifles.
Some would have knives.
From other countries and Jewish ones.
And they wanted my mother to join them.
But because of me, they couldn't take her.
Because they said they can't.
Because of a child, they can't have her with us.
So once again, she was left alone with me to just try and try.
And one time, we got to a big barn.
And we-- and the door was open.
And she crawled in with me.
And it was, like, dry.
It was like heaven, you know.
And we climbed up.
It was, like, a loft where they kept a lot of straw for the animals.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And it was-- she made, like, a bed for me.
And we was sleeping there.
And all of a sudden, she smelled something.
And she woke me up.
That was smoke.
The barn was on fire.
I remember that I didn't want to get up.
That was the first time I had something that was a little bit better than that terrible hole.
She says, the place is on fire.
We have to get out of here.
And we couldn't go down because everything was burning so much there.
But there was, like, a little doorway or a little window up on the top.
And she opened it up.
And she said, you hold my hand because we're going to jump.
And I says, well, I'm afraid to jump.
She says, we have to.
We have to jump or get burned alive in this place.
So we jumped.
And you know it's amazing-- didn't break a leg, didn't break an arm.
It's like somebody was looking over us-- and continued to run.
And, like, my whole childhood, that's all I knew.
I didn't know from being a child-- constant fear, constant hunger, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] thirst.
I don't know.
I don't know.
We woke up, and it was burning, burning so much-- so much fire.
It was just going right up-- the straw and the dry wood and everything was just-- [POINTS].
Yes.
We kept on running.
And we were in-- hiding behind cemetery places.
And I told you about the, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the, the place where the corn-- tall corn grows.
And we were hiding in places like that.
But then the Germans got on to us.
They started finding a lot of them in these places.
And they started looking in these places.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I'm talking now-- it doesn't seem like I was the one going through it.
But it was my mother.
I wish she was here to give you an account of the terrible things that she endured.
And she fought every time.
When they finally got someplace where she could find a little piece of bread or a little piece of something, she was so excited.
She would run with such excitement because she had something to give me.
[SOBBING] It's a mother's love.
[SOBBING] It never stopped.
[CRYING] [MOAN] A lot of my friends-- she was so protective of me only one of [SOBBING] I guess that my-- her whole world revolved around me.
And I was always her [?
Tamala. ?] That's me.
She had to protect 'my Tamale.' [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My brothers, who-- as I told you at the beginning, she was able to smuggle them out, thinking they would have a better chance.
And they were in hiding by themselves, two young kids.
And they were extremely hungry.
So they saw an apple tree.
And they climbed the tree to get an apple to have something to eat.
But one of the farmer boys saw them.
So they called the SS Gestapo.
And they came in their car.
And they shot them down as they were running.
The farm lady told us that one of them had a bite of the apple still on his mouth.
Yes, it was right outside Mlynow.
And this lady is-- she couldn't do anything to help them.
Because, like I told you, they threatened to kill them as well.
It was another little boy who lived not far away.
He-- see, they all knew that the Jews were hiding.
And they said that if you find that you a Jew, you tell us.
And they'll be rewarded for this.
That lady told us, the lady in the farm there.
After the war, after we were liberated.
We went back.
My mother was still searching to see if maybe my sister was still there.
We still kept going back.
And it was the same lady who found some of my sister's pictures on the ground.
And she knew of-- she knew my sister.
So she picked them up and took him home with her.
And after we got there, after we were liberated, she gave my mother pictures of my sister, which is the only thing I have from my previous life.
We were in a empty house.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was-- like, a few people were there.
And we were, like, in their basement.
Oh It was a bit after, could have been weeks.
I don't know.
One day went into another day.
And they all just bunched up together.
But I remember being there with my mother.
And we heard soldiers.
So naturally, we assumed it was the Germans that traced us down and found these clump of people who were Jewish people and, you know--
They were going to get rid of us, put us on the trucks.
Unoccupied house.
Probably about 10, about 10 of us were there.
Not in-- we were in the basement.
But we-- it was wooden floors.
And we heard boots, you know, walking, thinking that they have found us.
And they're going to come and get us.
That's the end of us.
And everybody started huddling already to say their goodbyes.
That's the end.
But then they heard voices from above in Russian.
And they said, don't be afraid.
We're not going to kill you.
And somebody said, that's a trick.
They only say that to get us out.
And then they'll get rid of us.
And he kept pleading, please, come up.
We're not going to kill you.
And little by little, somebody went up.
And then he yelled, they're not going to kill us!
They're not the Germans!
[CRYING] They're the Russians!
[SOBBING] And everybody fell to their feet and kissed their feet and kissed their hands.
[CRYING] Because we thought this is going to be-- they finally came to help us.
Somebody came to help us.
And they had big trucks.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they said, we're going to get you out of here and bring you back to your own places.
The trucks, I remember, had benches on each side of it.
And they had, like, a canvas top.
The back was open.
And we got up there.
And I-- my mother was sitting here.
And I sat across from my mother.
But they still had rifles.
And for some reason, one of the rifles discharged, just discharged.
Nobody aimed at anything.
And it landed right near my hair.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I think once again, somebody up there sort of covered to keep me safe.
And they took us back to our hometown.
And when we got there, my mother ran to find our house.
And it was impossible to find because the bombs had completely destroyed it.
It was nothing but bricks and wood and-- she knew the area.
But there was no house there at all.
Everything was gone, completely gone.
I, I looked.
I tried to see.
And my mother says, this was our home.
But to a child, this couldn't be a home.
I mean, it's nothing there.
It's all gone.
